does: This paper develops a morality dataset of 10.3K instances spanning Value Ambiguity and Value Conflict categories, then formalizes four adversarial attack strategies that exploit this dataset to manipulate LLMs' judgments on moral questions. The authors evaluate both standalone large language models and guardrail models deployed in generative systems, demonstrating that these models exhibit critical vulnerabilities to subtle, sophisticated morality-aware attacks that can jailbreak safety mechanisms and elicit harmful outputs.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature investigating jailbreak attacks against LLMs, extending prior research from prompt-based adversarial techniques to the specific domain of moral pluralism. It responds to recent efforts in pluralism alignment that aim to create LLMs capable of serving morally diverse humanity, while addressing gaps in understanding the robustness of these models against value-targeted attacks. The research combines insights from adversarial ML, AI safety and alignment research, and studies on LLM vulnerabilities, leveraging the observation that jailbreak prompts can reveal internal model behaviors and brittle points in safety training.
enables: This work points toward more rigorous evaluation frameworks for moral robustness in LLMs and the development of improved alignment techniques that account for pluralistic values under adversarial pressure. It enables future research into designing guardrail models resilient to morality-aware attacks and understanding how value conflicts and ambiguities can be exploited. The findings support the case for more sophisticated safety training methods that go beyond surface-level instruction following and build genuine robustness across diverse moral perspectives, ultimately informing the design of AI systems that maintain alignment even when faced with subtle but systematic attacks targeting their value judgments.
